title: Denoising Autoencoders vs. Plain Autoencoders
concept: Plain autoencoders can learn trivial identity mappings; denoising autoencoders cannot, because input and target are never identical.

exercises.prompt: What does an autoencoder try to do?

options.text: Copy its input and rebuild it as output

exercises.explain: An autoencoder squeezes input into a small code, then tries to rebuild the original from that code.

cards.title: The Lazy Copycat Problem
cards.body: Imagine a student who just traces a picture instead of learning to draw it. Looks perfect... but they learned nothing! Plain autoencoders can fall into this exact trap.

cards.title: Two Kinds of Autoencoders
cards.body: A plain autoencoder learns to rebuild the SAME input it was given. A denoising autoencoder is given a MESSY (corrupted) input but must rebuild the CLEAN original. Since input ≠ target, it can't just copy — it must actually understand the data.

cards.title: Tracing vs. Sketching from Memory
cards.body: A plain autoencoder is like tracing a photo — easy, but you don't really learn to draw. A denoising autoencoder is like being shown a smudged, blurry photo and asked to sketch the clear original from memory — that forces real understanding.

cards.title: Cleaning Up a Photo
cards.body: You feed a denoising autoencoder a photo with random static noise added. Its job: output the clean, noise-free photo. Because the noisy input never matches the clean target, the network must learn the true structure of faces, edges, and shapes — not just copy pixels.

cards.title: Myth: Autoencoders Always Learn Useful Features
cards.body: Not true! A plain autoencoder with too much capacity can cheat by learning an identity function — basically memorizing a shortcut to copy input to output without learning anything meaningful. Denoising forces it to actually generalize.

cards.title: Copy Machine vs. Detective
cards.body: Compare how each network treats its job.

left.text: Input = Target. Can shortcut by copying, risking trivial identity mapping.

right.text: Input = noisy, Target = clean. Must reconstruct real structure, no shortcuts possible.

exercises.prompt: Why can't a denoising autoencoder just copy its input to produce the output?

options.text: Because the input is corrupted/noisy and never matches the clean target
Because it has no output layer
Because it only works on text data
Because copying is against the rules of coding

exercises.explain: Since the noisy input is never identical to the clean target, simple copying can't produce a correct answer — the network must learn real patterns.

exercises.prompt: A plain autoencoder can sometimes learn a trivial identity mapping (just copying input to output) without learning anything useful.
exercises.explain: True! If given enough capacity, a plain autoencoder can cheat by simply passing the input straight through instead of learning meaningful features.

exercises.sentence: In a denoising autoencoder, the input is corrupted, but the target is always the ___ version of the data.
exercises.explain: The target is the clean, original version — the network must learn to reconstruct it from a noisy input.

exercises.prompt: Which setup FORCES a network to learn real patterns instead of just copying?

options.text: Feeding noisy input and asking for the clean output
Feeding the exact same input as the target
Giving the network unlimited capacity to memorize

exercises.explain: When input and target differ (like noisy vs. clean), the network cannot rely on a simple copy shortcut, so it must learn true structure.

exercises.prompt: A teacher gives two students the same worksheet. Student A copies the answer key word-for-word. Student B is given a smudged worksheet and must figure out the correct answers from context. Which student is like a denoising autoencoder, and why?

options.text: Student B, because they can't just copy — they must understand the material to reconstruct it
Student A, because copying is always the best learning strategy
Both students, because they get the same grade
Neither, because autoencoders don't learn from worksheets

exercises.explain: Student B matches a denoising autoencoder: since the given (smudged) version doesn't match the correct answer, real understanding is required — just like how noisy input forces genuine learning instead of trivial copying.
